summary: In this 2011 Turing Award interview, Judea Pearl discusses the foundations of his work on causality and counterfactuals. He explores the concept of free will as a computationally necessary illusion for social coordination, explains empathy as a cognitive shortcut for moral behavior, and advocates for a rebellious, restless approach to scientific inquiry.

principles.statement: Free will is an illusion, but a computationally necessary one for social communication and responsibility.
principles.rationale: Though we are deterministic machines, evolution equipped us with the illusion of free will because it allows us to communicate effectively, assign responsibility, and coordinate behavior in society.
principles.source_quote: My position is that there’s no free will, but it’s a very useful illusion... it’s a simple computational problem – would a robot team play better if we equipped them with that language, vocabulary of free will?

principles.statement: Empathy is a computational shortcut for predicting the behavior of other agents.
principles.rationale: Instead of building a complex new model for every person, we use our built-in model of ourselves as a first approximation to predict how others will react, which forms the basis of moral behavior.
principles.source_quote: The only way I can predict your action is to assume first – so a first degree of approximation – that you are like me... that will enable me to avoid the computational hardship of building a new model of yourself.

principles.statement: Rebellion and a restless mind are essential for scientific discovery.
principles.rationale: You must insist on understanding things your own way and refuse to accept standard answers that do not satisfy your curiosity.
principles.source_quote: Rebel, if you don’t understand and insist on understanding it your way, and until you understand it your way, don’t take a standard answer as an answer if it doesn’t satisfy the question that you have in mind.

principles.statement: Religious language is a powerful poetic tool for transmitting moral ideals.
principles.rationale: Even for a staunch atheist, religious metaphors (like God punishing you) are highly effective communication devices for teaching children morality and the long-term consequences of their actions.
principles.source_quote: What is God? It’s a collection of ideals that we would like to acquire and we would like to pass on to the next generation. That is God.

frameworks.name: Empathy-Based Prediction
frameworks.when_to_apply: When you need to predict another agent's behavior or determine a moral course of action without excessive computational overhead.
steps: Assume as a first approximation that the other agent is like you.
Input the environmental signal or proposed action into your own internal model.
Ask yourself: 'How would I react to this signal?' or 'Would I be hurt?'
Attribute that predicted reaction to the other agent to guide your behavior.
frameworks.source_quote: I already have a model of yourself. It’s me. So I imagine that I were you and I give you a signal, and I ask, 'Would I have reacted...? How would I react to this signal?' That’s the way I expect you to react.

mental_models.name: Free Will as a Communication Protocol
mental_models.description: Viewing free will not as a metaphysical reality, but as a necessary vocabulary for agents (human or robot) to assign blame, correct behavior, and coordinate in teams.
mental_models.example: Equipping a robot soccer team with the vocabulary of free will so they can tell each other, 'You should have passed this ball, and the reason we lost this game is because you haven’t done what you’re supposed to do.'

mental_models.name: God as a Poetic Metaphor
mental_models.description: Treating religious concepts as shorthand for collective ideals and long-term consequences, rather than literal truths.
mental_models.example: Telling a child 'God will punish you' as a poetic, easily understandable way of saying 'Eventually you're going to cause harm to yourself if you do that.'

heuristics: Every computer scientist is a frustrated psychologist.
If you don't understand it your way, don't take a standard answer as an answer.

signature_quotes.text: My position is that there’s no free will, but it’s a very useful illusion.
signature_quotes.context: When asked about his position on the illusion of free will.

signature_quotes.text: Every computer scientist is a frustrated psychologist.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why students are drawn to computer science to learn more about themselves by emulating human behavior.

signature_quotes.text: Rebellion pays. A restless mind pays.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on his career and giving advice to young scientists.

signature_quotes.text: What is God? It’s a collection of ideals that we would like to acquire and we would like to pass on to the next generation.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how he views religion and God as a staunch atheist.

anti_patterns.description: Fearing AI advancement and the idea that robots will take over.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Understanding what robots are capable of doing helps us understand what we are capable of doing, which ultimately benefits society by broadening the boundaries of curiosity.

anti_patterns.description: Accepting authority as a replacement for personal curiosity.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It stifles the restless, rebellious mind necessary for true scientific understanding and discovery. You must insist on understanding things your own way.

anti_patterns.description: Taking religious metaphors literally to justify harmful actions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates an ideology of terror where individuals believe they have divine permission to elevate their own needs and grievances above all accepted rules of right and wrong.
